fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the geo data library before the sprint ends. The core ask is pretty straightforward — we're building something that lets devs work with geographic shapes and interchange them as standard JSON without hand-rolling all that tedious parsing stuff every time. Think points, paths, areas, collections, the whole family. Each shape needs to be constructable from raw data, and we need proper validation so bad inputs blow up clearly rather than silently producing garbage — similar to how we handled the validation patterns in that coordinate module we did before, just apply the same philosophy here.

The serialization side needs to support both compact and pretty-printed output. We also need support for coordinate reference systems — named ones, linked ones (with URI validation), and the 'no CRS specified' case. Features and feature collections need to wrap geometries with optional IDs and property bags. Equality checks need to work reliably across all types, including hash code consistency so these can safely live in sets or maps.

One thing the business cares about specifically: when a consumer sends us bad data (null where something's required, rings that don't close properly, coordinate lists that are too short), they need to get back a clear, consistent machine-readable error code — not a stack trace or some runtime-specific exception message. The error shape needs to be the same regardless of what went wrong or which layer caught it. We also want a command-driven adapter so QA can black-box test this via JSON commands over stdin/stdout.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: when construction fails, we need those failures normalized into the same language-independent block every time. The set we’re using is error=argument_null + param=<name> for required arguments that are null, error=out_of_range + param=<name> for arguments outside allowed range, error=argument_invalid + param=<name> + reason=<must_be_specified|must_be_dereferenceable_uri|not_a_linear_ring|invalid> for other invalid arguments, error=json for unparseable input, and error=not_supported for unsupported conversions. Also worth being explicit here that the core domain must never write to stdout — only the adapter turns those into the output block, following the construction-time validation and error normalization contract defined in the Execution Adapter Protocol section of start.md.

Also, property order is not flexible here. A geometry renders 'type' first, then 'coordinates' (or 'geometries' for GeometryCollection), with any 'crs' member last. A CRS object renders 'properties' first, then 'type'. A Feature renders 'type', then optional 'id', then 'geometry', then 'properties'. A FeatureCollection renders 'type', then 'features', then optional 'crs'. On the command side, the make_crs operation prints first a line 'type=<name|link|unspecified>', then for property-bearing CRS types, one 'prop.<key>=<value>' line per property sorted alphabetically by key. For a named CRS: type=name, prop.name=<value>. For a linked CRS without type: type=link, prop.href=<value>. For a linked CRS with type: type=link, prop.href=<value>, prop.type=<value>. Unspecified CRS prints only the type line.

A couple geometry specifics too: elevation, meaning the third coordinate component, is optional. It only shows up in output if it was supplied in the input. When elevation is absent, only the horizontal and vertical components are emitted. All supplied components are always emitted, including those equal to zero. Axis order is always horizontal first, vertical second, elevation third. For Polygon validation, each ring has to be a closed linear ring, so it must have at least four positions and its first position must equal its last position. A null ring list yields error=argument_null with param=coordinates. Any ring that fails that closed linear ring check yields error=argument_invalid with param=coordinates and reason=not_a_linear_ring.

And for the LineString helper behavior, the is_closed operation should report is_closed=true when the first and last positions coincide. is_linear_ring=true when the line string is closed AND has at least four positions. Both booleans are printed, one per line, in that order.

trigger_keywords: linked CRS URI validation
what hrefs are accepted for linked CRS
relative URI vs absolute URI for CRS
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a linked CRS, the href must not be null (error=argument_null, param=href), must not be empty (error=argument_invalid, param=href, reason=must_be_specified), and must be a dereferenceable URI — URIs containing characters like '<' or '>' that make them non-dereferenceable are rejected with error=argument_invalid, param=href, reason=must_be_dereferenceable_uri. Relative references are accepted.

trigger_keywords: feature equality rules
what determines if two features are equal
does feature id affect equality
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Feature equality is determined solely by the wrapped geometry — not by id or properties. Features with equal geometries are equal even when their identifiers or properties differ. Two features with no geometry are equal. Comparing a feature to null returns not-equal and must not throw. hash_equal is 'na' when one or both sides are null.

trigger_keywords: unspecified CRS serialization
how does null CRS serialize
what happens when no CRS is provided
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An unspecified CRS (kind=unspecified) serializes as a literal null value for the 'crs' member on a FeatureCollection. When no CRS is supplied at all (CRS member absent from the input), the output object omits the 'crs' member entirely rather than writing null.

trigger_keywords: indented vs compact formatting
how does indented output look
formatting option for serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The serialize operation accepts a 'formatting' field of either 'none' (compact, no whitespace) or 'indented' (pretty-printed with newlines and 2-space indentation). For indented output, numbers that are integers in the input (e.g. 1, 2) are rendered as floats (e.g. 1.0, 2.0) in the coordinates array.

trigger_keywords: multipolygon null coordinates param name
what param name for null multipolygon list
error param for missing polygon list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a MultiPolygon is constructed with a null polygon list, the error is error=argument_null with param=polygons (not 'coordinates'). This differs from other geometry types which use param=coordinates.

trigger_keywords: roundtrip target types supported
what targets are valid for roundtrip
which type names can be parsed in roundtrip
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Valid roundtrip targets include: Point, LineString, MultiPoint, MultiLineString, Polygon, MultiPolygon, GeometryCollection, Feature, FeatureCollection, Geometry (type-driven geometry dispatch), GeoJSONObject (type-driven any object), FeaturePoint and FeatureLineString (feature over a known geometry type), FeatureTypedPoint (feature with typed property object), and FeatureCollectionGeneric (collection with typed feature properties).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'serialize', 'equals', 'is_closed', 'construct', 'roundtrip_equal', 'wrapper_roundtrip', 'make_crs', 'roundtrip', 'typed_feature_serialize'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The UnspecifiedCrs type (kind=unspecified) which serializes as a literal null crs member when explicitly set, versus the complete absence of a crs member when no CRS is provided at all — two distinct behaviors covered in features 14 and 15.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm from the ticket. You'd need to check the PRD or look at the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature — each feature file has concrete input/output examples that should clarify the expected behavior.

feature01_point.json.description: A point is the simplest geometry: a single coordinate. Serializing a point produces a JSON object carrying a type discriminator and a coordinate array given in axis order (horizontal first, then vertical, then an optional elevation as a third element). Every supplied coordinate component is always emitted, including components whose value is zero. Deserializing the same shape reconstructs an equal point, preserving the optional elevation only when it was present. Two points built from the same coordinate compare equal and produce the same hash code; differing coordinates compare unequal.

feature02_linestring.json.description: A line string is an ordered sequence of two or more coordinates. It serializes to a type discriminator plus an array of coordinate arrays, and round-trips back to an equal value. A line string can report whether it is closed (its first and last coordinates coincide) and whether it forms a linear ring (closed and having at least four coordinates). Construction validates its input: a null coordinate sequence is rejected as a missing required argument, and a sequence with fewer than two coordinates is rejected as out of range. Equal coordinate sequences yield equal line strings sharing a hash code.

feature03_multipoint.json.description: A multi-point is a collection of point geometries. It serializes to a type discriminator and an array of coordinate arrays, and round-trips back to an equal value. Two multi-points built from the same set of coordinates compare equal and share a hash code.

feature04_multilinestring.json.description: A multi-line-string is a collection of line strings. It serializes to a type discriminator and a nested array of coordinate arrays, and round-trips back to an equal value. Two multi-line-strings built from the same coordinates compare equal and share a hash code.

feature05_polygon.json.description: A polygon is described by one or more linear rings: an exterior ring optionally followed by interior rings. It serializes to a type discriminator and a nested array of coordinate arrays, and round-trips back to an equal value, including the multi-ring (exterior plus interior) case. Construction validates its input: a null ring sequence is rejected as a missing required argument, and any ring that is not a closed linear ring (at least four coordinates, first equal to last) is rejected as invalid. Polygons built from equal rings compare equal and share a hash code.

feature06_multipolygon.json.description: A multi-polygon is a collection of polygons. It serializes to a type discriminator and a deeply nested array of coordinate arrays, and round-trips back to an equal value. A null polygon sequence is rejected at construction as a missing required argument. Multi-polygons built from equal polygons compare equal and share a hash code.

feature07_geometrycollection.json.description: A geometry collection groups several geometries of any kind. It serializes to a type discriminator and an array of nested geometry objects, and round-trips back to an equal value. A null geometry sequence is rejected at construction as a missing required argument. Collections built from equal member geometries compare equal and share a hash code.

feature08_geometry_converter.json.description: Any geometry can be serialized and then read back through a converter that selects the concrete geometry kind from the type discriminator alone. For each geometry kind, serializing and then deserializing through this type-driven converter reconstructs a value equal to the original.

feature09_geojson_converter.json.description: A general converter can serialize and deserialize any top-level GeoJSON object purely from its type discriminator, without the caller naming the concrete type in advance. Round-tripping a geometry through this general converter reconstructs an equal value, whether the static reference type used is the common base type or the common interface.

feature10_serialization_formatting.json.description: Serialization honors the formatting setting of the serializer. With indented formatting the output contains line breaks; with compact formatting the output contains neither line breaks nor whitespace separators. The same geometry content is represented either way.

feature11_geometry_as_property.json.description: A geometry can be embedded as a typed property of an arbitrary host object and serialized as part of that object, using a converter that restores the correct geometry kind on read. For each geometry kind, serializing a host object that carries the geometry and then deserializing it yields a host object that compares equal to the original and shares its hash code.

feature12_named_crs.json.description: A named coordinate reference system identifies a CRS by name. It exposes a type marker indicating a named system and carries the name under a dedicated property key. When attached to a GeoJSON object it serializes as a nested object containing that property and the named-system type marker. Construction rejects a null name as a missing required argument and an empty name as invalid. Two named systems with the same name compare equal and share a hash code; different names compare unequal with differing hash codes.

feature13_linked_crs.json.description: A linked coordinate reference system references a CRS by dereferenceable URI, optionally with a link type. It exposes a type marker indicating a linked system and carries the href, and the optional type, under dedicated property keys. When attached to a GeoJSON object it serializes as a nested object and is read back from that same shape. Construction rejects a null href as a missing required argument, an empty href as invalid, and an href that is not a dereferenceable URI as invalid; a relative reference is accepted. Two linked systems with the same href compare equal and share a hash code; different hrefs compare unequal.

feature14_unspecified_crs.json.description: An unspecified coordinate reference system represents the explicit absence of a CRS. It exposes a type marker indicating an unspecified system. When attached to a collection it serializes as a null CRS member, and reading a collection whose CRS member is null produces an unspecified system. Two unspecified systems always compare equal and share a hash code.

feature15_default_crs.json.description: When no CRS is supplied, a GeoJSON object omits the CRS member entirely on serialization, and an object read from text that has no CRS member carries no CRS. When a named CRS is present on a geometry, it is serialized after the coordinates as a nested named-system object and is restored on read. The well-known default geographic CRS is just a named system carrying its canonical name.

feature16_feature_serialize.json.description: A feature wraps a single geometry together with an optional identifier and a property bag. Serializing a feature produces a type discriminator, the optional identifier, the nested geometry, and the properties object. Features carrying each kind of geometry serialize to the corresponding nested geometry shape; a feature with no supplied properties serializes with an empty properties object.

feature17_feature_deserialize.json.description: Reading a feature reconstructs its geometry, identifier, and properties from text and re-emits them in canonical form. A feature with properties keeps them; a feature without properties yields an empty property bag; a feature whose geometry member is null, or absent entirely, yields a feature with no geometry.

feature18_feature_properties.json.description: A feature's property bag can be supplied as a set of key/value pairs, which are carried through to serialization. When no property object is supplied, the feature still exposes an empty property bag rather than a missing one.

feature19_feature_equality.json.description: Feature equality is determined solely by the wrapped geometry: features with equal geometries are equal even when their identifiers or properties differ, and features with differing geometries are unequal. Comparing a feature to a null reference is well-defined and never throws, returning not-equal; two features that both have no geometry are equal; a feature with a geometry and one without are unequal.

feature20_generic_feature.json.description: A feature can be read against a known geometry type or a known strongly typed property shape, instead of the open property bag. Reading against a specific geometry type or a typed property object reconstructs the feature and re-emits it canonically; serializing a feature with a typed property object emits those named properties. Comparing such a typed feature to a null reference is well-defined and returns not-equal.

feature21_feature_collection.json.description: A feature collection groups any number of features. It serializes to a type discriminator and an array of features, and reading a collection reconstructs every feature, including the kind of each member geometry; a collection may also be read against a strongly typed property shape applied to each feature. A null feature list is rejected at construction as a missing required argument. Collections built from equal features compare equal and share a hash code.
